article_title: China's FAST Telescope Finds a Millisecond Pulsar in an Almost Perfectly Circular Orbit, a Rare Window Into Binary Evolution

content_preview.title: China's FAST Telescope Finds a Millisecond Pulsar in an Almost Perfectly Circular Orbit, a Rare Window Into Binary Evolution
content_preview.summary: PSR J1810-0623 spins 220 times a second and circles a white dwarf every 15.4 days on an orbit with an eccentricity of just 0.000015, one of the roundest pulsar binaries known.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using China's Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope (FAST) have discovered a millisecond pulsar locked in an almost perfectly circular orbit around a white dwarf, a configuration so rare that it offers a fresh testing ground for theories of how binary star systems evolve. The pulsar, designated PSR J1810-0623, has a rotation period of just 4.55 milliseconds, meaning it spins about 220 times every second, [according to a Science China Press release distrib...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (597 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / Why It Matters / What We Don't Know sections. Technical depth is appropriate and accessible, and the 'What We Don't Know' section honestly bounds the claims (notes the white dwarf identity is only 'likely', and that the underlying paper's detailed timing/mass-function/dispersion-measure data lie beyond the press summaries).
editor_notes.source_verification: Two sources. source-1.html.gz (Universe Today, 'FAST Finds a Pulsar in an Almost Flawless Circular Orbit', Mark Thompson, June 15 2026, HTTP 200) is a complete, content-rich snapshot and was read in full from disk; its uncompressed sha256 matches the manifest (68e805ac...). It confirms: PSR J1810-0623 designation, ~220 spins/second, FAST as 'the largest single dish radio telescope on Earth, a vast bowl nestled into a natural hollow in the hills of southwest China', the bicycle-wheel recycling analogy, 'recycled with remarkable thoroughness', magnetic field decayed to 'a hundred million Gauss', carbon-oxygen white dwarf ~two-thirds of a solar mass, 15.4-day orbit, eccentricity ~0.000015 'rounder than almost any comparable system known, even beating the celebrated PSR J1614-2230', and the 'natural laboratory to weigh the neutron star, test theories of how binaries evolve, and even probe gravity itself' quote. All Universe Today quotes in the article are verbatim. source-0.html.gz (EurekAlert / Science China Press release, HTTP 202) captured only an AWS WAF bot-challenge (CAPTCHA) page, not the release text; its sha256 matches the manifest (22d5195e...) but the content is unusable for verification. As a last resort I WebFetched the live EurekAlert URL and verified every claim attributed to the release verbatim: 'rotation period of just 4.55 milliseconds', 'spins about 220 times every second', eccentricity 'only about 0.000015', 'orbit their common center of mass every 15.4 days', 'carbon-oxygen white dwarf' of 'about 0.64 times that of the Sun', 'an orbit so close to circular that its elliptical shape is almost undetectable', 'material from the companion star falls onto the neutron star, transferring angular momentum and causing it to spin faster', 'moderate-mass X-ray binary system', surface magnetic field 'has also decayed to only about 100 million Gauss', 'providing a new observational benchmark for testing binary evolution theories', 'precise observations spanning six and a half years', publication in Science China Physics, Mechanics & Astronomy dated 11-Jun-2026, and expert contact 'Jie Zhang, School of Arts and Sciences, Shanghai Dianji University'. All twelve checked specifics matched the live release exactly.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific (4.55 ms / ~220 Hz spin, eccentricity 0.000015, 15.4-day orbit, ~0.64 solar-mass C-O white dwarf, ~100 million Gauss field, six-and-a-half-year observation span, June 11 2026 publication, the PSR J1614-2230 comparison) traces to a cited source and is reproduced accurately. The paywalled Springer primary paper is correctly kept OUT of article.sources and no specific is attributed to it; every number is carried by EurekAlert or Universe Today. The 'moderate-mass X-ray binary system' phrasing is correctly attributed to 'what the release calls' with the exact press-release wording (the bot did not import the paper-title phrase 'intermediate-mass binary'). No hallucinations detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism, no editorializing, no AI self-reference. 'Rare' and 'exceptional' framing is grounded in the sources' own language.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior Machine Herald coverage of PSR J1810-0623 or this FAST discovery. Closest prior astronomy pieces (2026-04/30 Markarian 501 binary black hole; 2026-06/12 ASKAP J1745-5051 white-dwarf binary radio transient) cover different objects and events; this is not a duplicate. ASKAP J1745-5051 noted as a related-articles link (adjacent compact-object-binary coverage).
concerns: EurekAlert snapshot (source-0.html.gz) captured an AWS WAF bot-challenge page rather than the release body (HTTP 202). The snapshot is therefore unusable for offline verification; claims attributed to the release were verified against the live URL via WebFetch instead, with all twelve checked specifics matching verbatim. The provenance snapshot for this source does not contain the release text.

The article renders the pulsar designation as 'PSR J1810-0623' (ASCII hyphen) while both sources use the Unicode minus sign 'PSR J1810−0623'. This is the standard ASCII transliteration of the same J2000 designation and does not change the object identified; flagged only for completeness, not as an error.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Accurate, neutral, well-sourced News report. Every specific and every direct quote was verified verbatim against a cited source (Universe Today from the committed snapshot; EurekAlert from the live URL after its snapshot returned a WAF challenge). Headline, summary, and lead are all source-backed, both body URLs are present in article.sources, and the paywalled primary paper is correctly excluded with no specific cited to it. No issues that a corrections note would need to cover.
